meta.title: E-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes • E-Class • 1993–present • 5 Generations • OM651 • OM642 • OM654 • M276
hero.h1: E-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every E-Class engine — from the W210's M104 straight-six to the W214's electrified OM654 and M254 — covered across five generations. Real UK replacement costs, known failure patterns including OM651 "Black Death" and OM642 oil cooler seals, and honest repair-vs-replace economics. Backed by 1,018+ real UK E-Class engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ✅ The E-Class rule — generation is destiny: post-2016 OM654 cars are straightforward keeps; pre-2016 OM651 cars demand documented history, not just low miles.
OM654 — the modern diesel with materially fewer failure reports than its OM651 predecessor [MB-VERIFIED]
OM651 — "Black Death" injector seal risk plus rear-chain complexity; buy with documented history, not a "bargain" price
W212 facelift (2013–2016) with the OM654 diesel — post-2013 electronics, facelift styling, and the more dependable engine before W213 depreciation fully hits
OM651 injector seal failure ("Black Death") — 1,250 enquiries across all Mercedes models in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]
🥇 #1 Best-Selling Mercedes — the E-Class is historically Mercedes' best-selling model globally, with more than 13 million sold by 2015

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The E-Class is the safest bet in the Mercedes range — buy a post-2016 OM654 car if your budget allows, or a meticulously documented facelift W212 if it doesn't. The generation matters more than the mileage.

rows.value: Diesel, Petrol, Petrol Hybrid, Diesel Hybrid
OM651 — "Black Death" injector seal failure, rear timing chain

generations.subHeadline: Five generations, one nameplate — from the W124 that launched the E-Class badge to the all-new W214. Each has a clear buy/avoid verdict.

cards.title: W124 (1984–1995) (pre‑1993 cars carried the "E" as a suffix, e.g. 300 E; the E‑Class badge formally appeared on the 1993 facelift)
cards.verdict: The car that made the E-Class name. Classic Mercedes build quality, but now 30+ years old — only buy as a collector or weekend car, not a daily driver. Parts availability is thinning.

cards.title: W210 (1995–2003)
cards.meta: 1995–2003 • M104 inline-six, M119 V8, OM606 diesel
cards.verdict: The "Eyes" E-Class — distinctive quad-headlamps, but rust is the real enemy now, not the engines. Low values mean most engine replacements fail the economics test. Buy only if rust-free and fully documented.

cards.title: W211 (2002–2009)
cards.meta: 2002–2009 • OM642 V6 diesel, M272 V6 petrol, M113 V8
cards.verdict: The E-Class that defined the 2000s executive market. The OM642 is the pick of the bunch — well-understood, labour-intensive but not catastrophic. SBC brake system and Airmatic suspension are the bigger wallet-drainers than the engines.

cards.title: W212 (2009–2016)
cards.meta: 2009–2016 • OM651 four-cylinder diesel, OM642 V6 diesel, M276 V6 petrol
cards.verdict: The sweet spot for value. The 2013 facelift brought the most complex mid-cycle refresh Mercedes ever attempted — crucially, it brought the OM654 diesel into the range. Buy 2013+ with the OM654; avoid the OM651 without full chain history.

cards.title: W213 (2016–2023)
cards.meta: 2016–2023 • OM654 diesel, M276 V6 petrol, OM656 straight-six diesel, M177 V8 (AMG)
cards.verdict: The modern high-water mark. The OM654 is the defining engine — materially fewer failures than the OM651, AdBlue issues are software-manageable. The W213 is the safest used E-Class buy if your budget stretches to it.

cards.title: W214 (2023–present)
cards.meta: 2023–present • OM654 (mild-hybrid diesel), M254 (mild-hybrid petrol), plug-in hybrids
cards.verdict: The all-new E-Class — fully electrified powertrain line-up, all inline-four configurations. Too new for long-term reliability data, but the OM654's track record suggests it's a safe bet. Premium pricing reflects the new-generation tech.

engineDatabase.h2: E-Class Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to a UK-market E-Class — verified against manufacturer data, with real 2025 enquiry volumes and replacement costs.

commonProblems.subHeadline: Six real failure patterns from UK E-Class owners — ranked by severity, with genuine cost signals.

problems.description: Copper injector seals degrade, leaking combustion gases past the seal into a carbon "tar" that can weld the injector into the head if ignored. The defining E-Class diesel issue — our #1 enquiry driver across all Mercedes models. Most common on W212, less frequent on post-2013 facelift cars. Repair: £400–£1,200; full engine replacement if neglected: £2,800–£4,800 [MB-QUOTE].

problems.issue: OM651 Rear Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.description: Unlike most engines, the OM651's chain sits at the transmission end — access requires separating the engine from the gearbox. Warm-start rattle is the early warning; ignore it and the chain can skip, destroying the engine. Chain replacement: £600–£1,500; full engine if failed: £2,800–£4,800 [MB-QUOTE].

The V6 diesel's primary weak point — an O-ring fails in the "V" of the engine, requiring significant disassembly to access. Oil leaks into the engine "V" and drains down, causing oil loss and potential engine damage if ignored. Repair: £800–£1,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Most common on W211 and early W212.

Timing chain stretch on earlier M276 examples, typically appearing after 100,000–150,000 km. Symptoms include rattling noise on start-up, loss of power, and valve timing errors. Chain replacement: £1,500–£2,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Most common on W212 and early W213.

The modern diesel's most common complaint — NOx sensor failures and AdBlue heater faults. These are software-manageable rather than mechanical, but can trigger limp mode if ignored. AdBlue repairs: £400–£1,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Most common on W213.

W211 and W212 Airmatic systems are a more common wallet-drainer than the engines themselves. Compressor failure or air-strut leaks cause the car to sit low on one corner. Not an engine problem — but frequently the reason owners scrap otherwise sound cars. Repair: £1,500–£3,000 per corner.

marketIntelligence.h2: E-Class Market Intelligence — What 1,018+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: Interesting insight: The W212 (2009–2016) generates 338 enquiries — nearly 60% more than the newer W213 (214 enquiries). This is classic "sweet spot" behaviour: the W212 is old enough to need repairs, young enough to be worth fixing, and cheap enough to be accessible to a wider audience than the W213.

The W211's 234 enquiries reflect its status as the last "analogue" E-Class before Mercedes' more complex electronics arrived — mechanically straightforward, well-understood, and cheaply parted. The OM642 V6 is the pick of the generation, but Airmatic suspension is the real cost driver.

The OM654's 520 enquiries across all Mercedes models are weighted toward routine maintenance and pre-purchase inspections, not catastrophic failure — a materially different profile from the OM651's "Black Death" and chain-related enquiry mix. The shift in enquiry character is as significant as the volume.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every E-Class Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you buy one E-Class based on this page, buy a 2013–2016 W212 facelift with the OM654 diesel. The facelift brought the more reliable engine and the most comprehensive mid-cycle refresh Mercedes has ever done — and values have already absorbed the steepest depreciation. Avoid the OM651 without full timing chain history; the £2,000 you save on the purchase price can become a £4,800 engine replacement. End of Sections 1–7. Ready for "8-14" to continue with Replacement Costs, Engine Evolution, Who Should Buy, Calculator CTA, Trust, FAQ, and Closing Action Cards.

replacementCosts.h2: E-Class Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt E-Class engines — supply-only, with independent labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an E-Class engine typically takes 8–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The E-Class rule — generation is destiny: A £3,000–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £14,000–£35,000 W213 sits at roughly 15–35% — well below our 70% threshold for Mercedes-Benz. A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £5,000–£18,000 W211 or early W212 sits at 30–110% — check the specific car's value before committing. The threshold for Mercedes-Benz is 70%: if the reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For a W211 E320 CDI worth £2,500 with a £4,500 recon bill, that's 180% — walk away. For a W213 E220d worth £18,000 with a £3,500 recon bill, that's 19% — fix it.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Class engine typically takes 8–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. The E-Class rule — generation is destiny: A £3,000–£5,000 OM654 recon against a £14,000–£35,000 W213 sits at roughly 15–35% — well below our 70% threshold for Mercedes-Benz. A £3,200–£5,500 OM642 recon against a £5,000–£18,000 W211 or early W212 sits at 30–110% — check the specific car's value before committing. The threshold for Mercedes-Benz is 70%: if the reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. For a W211 E320 CDI worth £2,500 with a £4,500 recon bill, that's 180% — walk away. For a W213 E220d worth £18,000 with a £3,500 recon bill, that's 19% — fix it.

engineEvolution.h2: How the E-Class Engine Range Evolved — 1993 to Today

eras.era: The W124 Era — Fuel Injection Arrives
eras.whyBmwChanged: The "E" in E-Class stood for Einspritzmotor (German for fuel injection engine) — the badge formalised in 1993 after years of "E" suffix usage on models like the 300 E
eras.worthKnowing: The W124 is widely considered the last "over-engineered" Mercedes — built to a standard, not a price. Values for well-preserved examples are rising.

eras.era: The W210 Era — V6 and Common-Rail
eras.keyEngines: M104 inline-six, M119 V8, OM606 diesel
eras.whyBmwChanged: The W210 introduced the first V6 petrol (M112) and the first common-rail diesel (OM611) to the E-Class range
eras.worthKnowing: Rust is the W210's defining failure, not the engines. A rust-free W210 with the OM606 diesel is a cult classic.

eras.era: The W211 Era — V6 Diesel Dominance
eras.keyEngines: OM642 V6 diesel, M272 V6 petrol, M113 V8
eras.whyBmwChanged: The OM642 arrived in 2005 and became the defining E-Class diesel of the era — 3.0 litres, V6, smooth and powerful
eras.worthKnowing: The OM642's oil cooler seal is the only real engine weakness — labour-intensive but not catastrophic. SBC brakes and Airmatic are bigger cost drivers.

eras.era: The W212 Era — Four-Cylinder Diesel Volume
eras.keyEngines: OM651 four-cylinder diesel, OM642 V6 diesel, M276 V6 petrol
eras.whyBmwChanged: The OM651 arrived in 2008 as Mercedes' new modular four-cylinder diesel — high-volume, efficient, but carrying the "Black Death" injector seal risk
eras.worthKnowing: The 2013 facelift was the most complex mid-cycle refresh Mercedes ever undertook — it brought the OM654 into the range. A 2013 car and a 2012 car have completely different reliability profiles.

eras.era: The W213 Era — The OM654 Takes Over
eras.keyEngines: OM654 diesel, OM656 straight-six diesel, M276 V6 petrol, M177 V8 (AMG)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The OM654 replaced the OM651 as Mercedes' modern four-cylinder diesel — materially fewer failures, AdBlue issues are software-manageable
eras.worthKnowing: The W213 is the safest used E-Class buy if your budget stretches to it. The OM656 straight-six is a gem — smooth, powerful, and refined.

eras.keyEngines: OM654 (mild-hybrid diesel), M254 (mild-hybrid petrol), plug-in hybrids
eras.whyBmwChanged: All W214 engines are electrified — 48-volt mild-hybrid as standard, with plug-in hybrid options across the range
eras.worthKnowing: Too new for long-term reliability data, but the OM654's track record suggests it's a safe bet. The W214 is the most technologically advanced E-Class ever built.

profiles.verdict: The E-Class estate (S212/S213) is one of the best family cars ever made — huge boot, comfortable ride, and the OM654 diesel returns excellent economy. Buy a 2016+ W213 estate with the OM654 and full service history.

The E-Class is built for motorway miles. The OM654 diesel returns 50+ mpg, the ride is supremely comfortable, and the W212/W213 interiors are quiet and refined. The 2013+ W212 facelift with the OM654 is the value sweet spot.

The W211 with the OM642 V6 diesel is the budget E-Class to buy — values are low, the engine is well-understood, and parts are plentiful. But factor in the £800–£1,500 oil cooler seal job and SBC brake costs before committing.

The E-Class estate and the OM654/OM656 diesels are exceptional for towing — 2,000 kg braked towing capacity, 500+ Nm of torque, and the 9G-Tronic gearbox handles load effortlessly. The W213 with the OM656 straight-six is the pick.

The W213 with the OM654 diesel is the E-Class to keep for 10+ years. The engine is proven, the platform is robust, and depreciation has already done the heavy lifting. Buy a 2019+ car with the facelift tech and full Mercedes service history.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My E-Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The OM651's "Black Death" injector seal failure is the defining E-Class diesel question — a £400–£1,200 repair if caught early, or a £2,800–£4,800 replacement if ignored. The OM642's oil cooler seal is a £800–£1,500 labour-intensive repair, but the engine itself is sound. Run the numbers on your specific car before deciding.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: When a £4,500 OM642 recon exceeds 70% of a £2,500 W211's value, we say so. That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

signals.text: mercedesengine.uk is part of the Engine Finders family, the UK's leading engine replacement marketplace — giving you the research depth of an independent authority with a vetted route to action when you need one.

items.question: How much does an E-Class engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A used E-Class engine costs £500–£5,000 depending on generation and engine code; reconditioned units range from £1,200–£14,000 [MB-QUOTE]. Labour adds 8–22 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). For a precise quote on your specific E-Class, use our diagnostic calculator.

items.question: Which E-Class engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The OM654 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel (2016–present) is the most reliable modern E-Class engine. It has materially fewer failure reports than its OM651 predecessor, and AdBlue issues are software-manageable rather than mechanical. 520 enquiries across all Mercedes models in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED]. The OM656 straight-six diesel is also highly regarded but has lower enquiry volume [PATTERN DATA].

items.question: Which E-Class engine should I avoid?
items.answer: The OM651 (2008–2016) carries a real, well-documented caveat: "Black Death" injector seal failure and rear timing chain tensioner wear. It's not a reason to avoid the engine entirely — with full service history, it's a solid unit — but it's a reason to demand documented history and a pre-purchase inspection. The highest-risk examples are pre-2013 W212 cars without timing chain history.

items.question: Is the E-Class timing chain problem real?
items.answer: Yes — on the OM651, the timing chain sits at the transmission end, requiring engine-gearbox separation for access. Warm-start rattle is the early warning; ignore it and the chain can skip, destroying the engine. Chain replacement: £600–£1,500; full engine if failed: £2,800–£4,800 [MB-QUOTE]. On the M276 petrol V6, timing chain stretch can occur on earlier examples — listen for rattling on start-up.

items.question: Is it worth replacing an E-Class engine?
items.answer: Apply the 70% Mercedes-Benz rule: if reconditioned engine cost exceeds 70% of what your car is worth, get a scrap valuation alongside any repair quote. A £3,200 OM642 recon against an £8,000 W212 is 40% — worth doing. A £4,500 OM642 recon against a £2,500 W211 is 180% — walk away. Use our Ownership Decision Tool for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Which E-Class generation/year should I buy?
items.answer: The definitive answer: a 2013–2016 W212 facelift with the OM654 diesel. The facelift brought the more reliable engine, the most comprehensive mid-cycle refresh Mercedes has ever done, and values have already absorbed the steepest depreciation. If your budget stretches further, the W213 (2016–2023) with the OM654 is the safest modern buy. Avoid pre-2013 W212 OM651 cars without full timing chain history.

items.question: How do I find my E-Class engine code?
items.answer: The engine code is stamped on the engine block — typically on the left-hand side (driver's side in RHD cars) near the cylinder head. It's also on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in your service documentation. Common E-Class codes: OM651, OM642, OM654, M276, OM656, M254. If you're unsure, use our diagnostic calculator to identify it.

items.question: E-Class vs C-Class — which is more reliable?
items.answer: Both carry a ✅ Safe verdict on their newer generations, but for different reasons. The E-Class W213 benefits from a wider, well-supported engine range across OM654, OM656 and M276 — and the E-Class is built to a higher standard of refinement than the C-Class. The C-Class W206 runs the OM654-only diesel range. Buy on generation and service history first, badge second. For a full comparison, see our E-Class vs C-Class guide.
